coredata.dc:title: One pot synthesis of carbon dots decorated carboxymethyl cellulose- hydroxyapatite nanocomposite for drug delivery, tissue engineering and Fe3+ ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Sarkar, Chandrani
Chowdhuri, Angshuman Ray
Kumar, Amit
Laha, Dipranjan
Garai, Subhadra
Chakraborty, Jui
Sahu, Sumanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots conjugated carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite has been synthesized by one-pot synthesis method and used for multiple applications like metal ion sensing, osteogenic activity, bio-imaging and drug carrier. The structure and morphology of the nanocomposite were systematically characterized by FTIR, XRD, TGA, FESEM, TEM and DLS. Results clearly demonstrated the formation of fluorescent enabled carbon dots conjugated nanocomposite from carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite by a simple thermal treatment. The synthesized nanocomposite is smaller than 100 nm and exhibits fluorescence emission band around 440 nm upon excitation with 340 nm wavelength. In the meantime, the nanocomposite was loaded with a chemotherapeutic drug, doxorubicin to evaluate the drug loading potential of synthesized nanocomposite. Moreover, the as-synthesized nanocomposite showed good osteogenic properties for bone tissue engineering and also exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydroxyapatite
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Carbon dots
One pot method
Nanocomposite

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot decorated carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite (CDs-CMC-HA)
Synthesis of hydroxyapatite (HA) nanoparticle and carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite (CMC-HA) nanocomposite
In-vitro MDA-MB-468 cell imaging by CDs-CMC-HA
Anticancer activity assay of DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA
Biocompatibility and osteogenic evaluation of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite
In-vitro DOX loading/release study and anticancer activity assay
In-vitro cell viability and cell differentiation study
Sensing of Fe3+ ions with CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: CouncilÂ forÂ Scientific and Industrial Research, South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge In house project support group (iPSG) (OLP-0231), CSIR- National Metallurgical Laboratory for financial support and research facilities. The authors also like to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines) [IIT-ISM], Dhanbad for providing characterization facilities. Author (Chandrani Sarkar) acknowledges University Grants Commission, India for granting Fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of synthetic procedure of carbon dots decorated carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite (CDs-CMC-HA) for doxorubicin delivery.
(a) FTIR spectra, (c) XRD patterns and (d) TGA curves of HA, CMC-HA, and CDs-CMC-HA [(b) FTIR spectrum of CMC].

FESEM and HRTEM images of synthesized (a,b) CMC-HA and (d,e) CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposites (inset: corresponding high magnification TEM images, scale bar 20nm); DLS spectra of (c) CMC-HA and (f) CDs-CMC-HA.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra of HA, CMC-HA and CDs-CMC-HA; (b) PL spectra of CDs-CMC-HA at different exitations (300 nm–390nm); (b) digital images of HA, CMC-HA and CDs-CMC-HA under UV light.
Fluorescence microscopy image of MDA-MB-468 cells after (a) 15min, (b) 30min, (c) 1h, and (d) 2h of incubation with CDs-CMC-HA [each scale bar represents 10μm].
(a) in-vitro DOX release profile of CDs-CMC-HA; (b) cytotoxicity evaluation of MDA-MB-468 cells exposed to CDs-CMC-HA and DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA at various concentration (0–100μg.mL−1) for 24h.

(a) in-vitro cell vaibility of MG63 cells cultured with 100μgmL−1 of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite for 1day, 4days and 7 days. (b) Alkaline phosphatase activity of MG63 cells cultured with osteogenic agents (positive control) and CDs-CMC-HA for 7days and 14 days; Images of mineralized matrix of MG63 cells after in-vitro culture with osteogenic agents and CDs-CMC-HA for 7 days, 14days and 21days determined by (c) ARS (d) VKS (magnification: 10×).

(a) Fluoroscent sensing behaviour of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposites in presence of different metal ions; (b) F/F0 plot of different metal ions; (c) Fluoroscent response of synthesized nanocomposites to different concentration of Fe3+ ion (0μM–10μM); (d) F0/F plot of different concentration of Fe3+ ion (Inset shows the linner relationship between F0/F and concentration of Fe3+ ion).

head.ce:title: One pot synthesis of carbon dots decorated carboxymethyl cellulose- hydroxyapatite nanocomposite for drug delivery, tissue engineering and Fe3+ ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad, 826004, Jharkhand, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advance Material and Processing Division, CSIR-National Metallurgical Laboratory, Jamshedpur, 831007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology, Jadavpur University, 188, Raja S C Mallick Road, Kolkata, 700032, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Life Science and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Glass & Ceramic Research Institute, 196, Raja S.C. Mullick Road, Jadavpur, Kolkata, 700 032, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dots conjugated carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite has been synthesized by one-pot synthesis method and used for multiple applications like metal ion sensing, osteogenic activity, bio-imaging and drug carrier. The structure and morphology of the nanocomposite were systematically characterized by FTIR, XRD, TGA, FESEM, TEM and DLS. Results clearly demonstrated the formation of fluorescent enabled carbon dots conjugated nanocomposite from carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite by a simple thermal treatment. The synthesized nanocomposite is smaller than 100 nm and exhibits fluorescence emission band around 440 nm upon excitation with 340 nm wavelength. In the meantime, the nanocomposite was loaded with a chemotherapeutic drug, doxorubicin to evaluate the drug loading potential of synthesized nanocomposite. Moreover, the as-synthesized nanocomposite showed good osteogenic properties for bone tissue engineering and also exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose is the most abundant renewable polymer available in nature. Materials based on cellulose and its derivatives particularly carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) have been widely used in a variety of applications for their fascinating properties such as hydrophilicity, biodegradability, biocompatibility and ease of processibility (Chang & Zhang, 2011). Due to these properties, it has been extensively studied in biomedical field, ranging from tissue engineering to drug delivery (Camponeschi et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2015; Kono, 2014). Compared to pure form, cellulose-inorganic hybrid materials have attracted increasing attention for their superior properties such as less swelling degree, higher mechanical properties and exceptional bioactivity (He, Chang, Peng, & Zhang, 2012). So far, hydroxyapatite (HA) is the most appropriate inorganic material for this purpose due to its close similarity with inorganic component of natural bone; induces excellent bioactivity in the composites. Recently, researchers have introduced hydroxyapatite into carboxymethyl cellulose in an effective way to combine the advantages of cellulose and hydroxyapatite; and develop materials with high functionality (Fragal et al., 2016). Consequently, carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite (CMC-HA) composite has emerged as one of the most promising material in biomedical field (Pasqui, Torricelli, Cagna, Fini, & Barbucci, 2014; Azzaouia et al., 2017).

Currently, researchers have designed HA based nanocomposite and applied in multiple applications. For example, Azzaouia and co-authors synthesized a tri-functional CMC-HA composite and employed as (i) toxic material absorbent; (ii) antimicrobial and antifungal composite; and (iii) a candidate for bone tissue engineering (Azzaouia et al., 2017). In the present work, we have aimed to synthesize a CMC-HA composite for diverse applications such as in tissue engineering, drug delivery and metal ion sensing. To develop a smart drug carrier as well an intelligent ion sensor, it is essential to conjugate fluorescent molecules or particles with the composite.

Carbon dots (CDs), a luminescent nanomaterial has been extensively studied due to its low cost, facile synthesis, hydrophilicity, high photostability and excellent biocompatibility. In recent years, there is a growing interest in developing surface functionalized nanomaterials with CDs for drug delivery; so that it can be accurately traced in cells and tissues. In our previous work, we have demonstrated the CDs embedded nanoscale metal organic frameworks for potential drug delivery (Chowdhuri, Singh, Ghosh, & Sahu, 2016). Furthermore, CDs have excellent ability to detect various metal ions by taking the advantages of its surface functional groups. The fluorescent quenching behavior of CDs after combination with metal ions; is the main aspect to use CDs as a sensing probe (Lv et al., 2017). These properties motivate us to expand the application scopes of CMC-HA composite from bone tissue engineering to drug delivery and metal ion sensing; with the incorporation of CDs. In the past few years, groups of researchers worked separately to fabricate CDs conjugated HA and CDs conjugated cellulose for different applications. Zhao and co-authors synthesized CDs conjugated F-substituted hydroxyapatite for cellular imaging (Zhao, Shi, Fang, & Feng, 2015). Guo et al. reported biomimetic calcium phosphate/CDs composites for cell labeling and copper ions detection (Guo et al., 2016). Gogai and co-authors developed CDs decorated HA nanohybrid for bone tissue engineering (Gogai, Kumar, Mandal, & Karak, 2016). You et al. designed a transparent sunlight conversion film by the conjugation of CDs with CMC (You, Zhang, Liu, & Lei, 2016). Lv et al. synthesized a CDs-bacterial cellulose composite for Fe3+ ion detection (Lv et al., 2017). In most of the reported literatures, conjugation of CDs was a complex lengthy process. Recently, two groups of researchers showed the possibilities of direct carbonization of cellulose to high fluorescent CDs by a simple hydrothermal process (Wu, Li, Tan, Wu, & Liu, 2015; Li et al., 2017). It inspired us to synthesize CDs conjugated CMC-HA nanocomposite directly from CMC-HA nanocomposite through a simple thermal route. In this study, we have developed a one pot method to synthesize carbon dots decorated CMC-HA nanocomposite for applications in bone tissue engineering, drug delivery and metal ion sensing arena. Initially, we have synthesized CMC-HA nanocomposite through in-situ mineralization process and refluxed it at 140°C for 8h to get fluorescent enabling CDs conjugated CMC-HA nanocomposite; and subsequently applied for multiple biomedical applications.

It is well known that the excessive amount of Fe3+ ions in human body cause serious diseases like Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, liver, and kidney damage. In view of this issue, the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions are very important (Wang et al., 2016). The synthesized CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite showed excellent selectivity towards Fe3+ ions over other metal ions. In this study, we have introduced a novel CDs conjugated CMC-HA nanocomposite (CDs-CMC-HA) possess the following notable advantages − (1) synthesized through a cost effective one-pot method; (2) having excellent properties for drug loading, release and imaging; (3) bone regenerative property; and (4) good sensitivity for Fe3+ ions.

Sodium salt of carboxymethyl cellulose (degree of substitution 0.9), calcium nitrate tetra hydrate (>98% assay), di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate and liquid ammonia (>25% assay) were purchased from Merck, Mumbai, India. Anticancer drug doxorubicin and dialysis membrane (MWCO 3.5 KD) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate buffer solution (PBS), Dulbecco’s modified eagle’s medium (DMEM) were purchased from Himedia, India, and Hyclone, USA respectively. Millipore water was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot decorated carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite (CDs-CMC-HA)
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite, calcium nitrate [Ca(NO3)2.4H2O] and di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate [(NH4)2HPO4] were taken as precursor of Ca2+ and PO4 3−ions. Initially, 10mg of CMC was dissolved in 60mL of water. In the meantime, aqueous solutions of calcium nitrate (0.9M) and di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate (0.56M) were also prepared in different beakers. After that, 400μL calcium nitrate solution (0.9M) was added slowly into CMC solution and stirred. The Ca-CMC solution was made alkaline (pH∼10-11) by adding ammonia solution and stirred for half an hour. At the end, 800μL di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate solution (0.56M) was added dropwise into it and made alkaline by adding ammonia solution with continuous stirring for 2h. A white coloration was developed which was refluxed at 140°C for 8h. The final product CDs-CMC-HA was collected and neutralized by dialysis method then dried at 50°C in a vacuum oven for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hydroxyapatite (HA) nanoparticle and carboxymethyl cellulose-hydroxyapatite (CMC-HA) nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: For study purpose, HA and CMC-HA were also synthesized. HA nanoparticles were synthesized by previously reported methods with slight modification (Wang, Xu, Zhao, & Sahai, 2015). Firstly, aqueous calcium nitrate (0.9M) solution and di-ammonium hydrogen phosphate (0.56M) solution were prepared. Then, 800μL phosphate solution was added dropwise into 400μL of calcium solution with continuous stirring. Thereafter, aqueous ammonia solution was added into the above mixture to maintain the pH∼10-11 and aged overnight at 30°C. Finally, the precipitate was collected, washed and dried at 50°C in vacuum oven. For the synthesis of CMC-HA, same procedure was followed as in CDs-CMC-HA; except heating. Here, the white precipitate of CMC-HA was neutralized by dialysis method and dried at 50°C.

The functional groups of CMC, HA, and synthesized nanocomposites were determined by Fourier transform infrared [FTIR (Agilent Cary 660)] spectrometer using KBr pellet method. The crystalline phase of samples was recognized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern recorded on Phillips PW 1710 X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of HA and synthesized nanocomposites was carried out in STA PT 1600, LINSEIS, Germany instrument under N2 atmosphere and collected over 25–1200°C temperature range at 10°Cmin−1 heating rate. The microstructural characterization of nanocomposites has been carried out using Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Supra 55, Carl Zeiss, Germany). The morphology and size of nanocomposites were examined by using high resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM cm200, CX Philips JEOL 2100) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The characteristic emission spectra of as-synthesized nanocomposites were recorded by Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) decay of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite was performed on Fluoro Max-4 fluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France). The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of the nanocomposites were measured by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique using Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, UK instrument.

An anticancer drug, doxorubicin (DOX) was used to evaluate the drug loading and release ability of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite. For drug loading, 2.5mg of CDs-CMC-HA was added into 5mL DOX (600μgmL−1) solution. Then, nanocomposite was ultrasonically dispersed for 3min and kept in shaking incubator at 37°C for 12h. The supernatant of DOX solution was collected by centrifugation and DOX loaded nanocomposite was washed with water to eliminate the unbound DOX molecules. The drug loading content (DLC) and drug loading efficiency (DLE) were calculated by following equations.
D L C ( % ) =
W e i g h t
o f
d r u g
i n
n a n o c o m p o s i t e s
W e i g h t
o f
n a n o c o m p o s i t e s
t a k e n
× 100
D L E ( % ) =
W e i g h t
o f
d r u g
i n
n a n o c o m p o s i t e s
W e i g h t
o f
t o t a l
i n j e c t e d
× 100

To examine the release rate of DOX from CDs-CMC-HA, 2.5mg of DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA were dispersed in 5mL PBS (pH 7.4) and incubated at 37°C for different time interval. After predetermined time interval, the aliquot was centrifuged then 1mL of suspension was withdrawn and replenished with same volume of fresh PBS. The amount of DOX release at regular time interval was quantified by checking the absorbance at 481nm from standard curve of free DOX.

Human triple negative breast cancers cells (MDA-MB-468) and osteoblast-like MG63 cells were procured from NCCS Pune, India. Cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, 2mgmL−1 NaHCO3, 1μgmL−1 penicillin G, 1mgmL−1 streptomycin in a humidified incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2. All the test materials were UV sterilized for 10min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro MDA-MB-468 cell imaging by CDs-CMC-HA
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to treatment with CDs-CMC-HA, MDA-MB-468 cells were grown on cover slip of about 70–80% confluence. On the day of study, the media was replaced with CDs-CMC-HA extract (5μgmL−1 in DMEM) and incubated for different time period (15min, 30min, 1h and 2h). After incubation, the extract was discarded and three times washed with PBS in order to remove excess CDs-CMC-HA. Finally images were captured using Fluorescence microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer activity assay of DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA
ce:section.ce:para: Anticancer activity of CDs-CMC-HA and DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA on MDA-MB-468 cells was studied. In this assay, MDA-MB-468 cells at a density of 1×104 cells per well were seeded in 96-well plate; and treated with CDs-CMC-HA and DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA for 24h at different concentrations (5, 10, 15, 25, 50 and 100μgmL−1) with a control experiment. After that, cells were washed with PBS followed by addition of 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (0.2mgmL−1) and incubated for 4h at 37°C. Finally, absorbance was measured at 595nm by microplate reader (BIORAD) and compared with the result of control well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and osteogenic evaluation of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was employed to quantitatively determine the cell viability and proliferation of MG63 cells, after exposure of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite. Initially, MG63 cells were seeded in 6-well plate at a density of 6×104 cells per well. After 24h, media of well plate was replaced with CDs-CMC-HA (100μgmL−1) extract and incubated for 1day, 4days and 7 days. At the end of incubation period, MTT solution (0.2mgmL−1) was added to each well and kept in incubator for 4h. During this period, the active mitochondrial dehydrogenase of live cells reduced the tetrazolium salt to formazan crystals. The developed formazan crystals were solubilized in DMSO (dimethyl sulphoxide) and absorbance was measured at 595nm by using microplate reader (BIORAD).

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity of MG63 cells (6×104 cells per well) cultured with osteogenic supplements and CDs-CMC-HA (100μgmL−1) extract was quantified by using ALP estimation kit of HiMedia (Product code- CCK035). Cell lysis was collected after incubation for 7days and 14days. 20μL of these cell lysates were added into ρ-nitrophenyl phosphate (ρ-NPP) containing assay buffer as per manufacture’s instruction. The absorbance was recorded at 405nm for estimating the ALP activity of cell.

Extracellular mineralization ability of CDs-CMC-HA was examined by alizarin red staining (ARS) and von kossa staining (VKS). Before adding sample extract (100μgmL−1) and osteogenic media, same number of cells as said above was seeded in well plate. After 24h, media with osteogenic supplements (positive control) and CDs-CMC-HA were added in respective wells; and incubated for 7days, 14days and 21days. Afterwards, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min and washed with biological water. Then, 1mL alizarin solution (2wt%) was supplied into each well and incubated for 30min. The images of mineralized nodules were captured by using inverted phase contrast microscope (MOTIC AE31). Same procedure was followed for von kossa staining except the using of alizarin solution. After cell fixation, 1mL silver nitrate solution (1wt%) was supplied into each well and kept under UV light for 30min. After that, images were captured by using inverted phase contrast microscope.

Fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ ions was conducted in deionized water at room temperature. For that, 1mg of CDs-CMC-HA was dispersed in 2mL of deionized water and desired concentration (0–10μM) of Fe3+ ions were added into it. The resulting solution was gently shaken and incubated for 15min at room temperature. At the end, fluorescence spectra of prepared solutions were recorded under excitation at 340nm. To ensure the selectivity of Fe3+ ions, the same procedure was followed with different metal ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, K+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Al3+ and Ni2+) solution in absence of Fe3+ ions.

Measured data were expressed as mean and standard deviation with n=3. Statistical analysis was performed by using GraphPad Prism with student’s t test and one way ANOVA; the value of p <0.05 is statistically significant.

In this study, carboxymethyl cellulose has been established as an active functional material due to its recognizable contribution in in-situ mineralization of HA and the formation of high fluorescent CDs through carbonization. The schematic presentation of the starting CMC-HA formation step followed by CDs-CMC-HA formation to drug loading and release behavior of CDs-CMC-HA are shown in Fig. 1 .

The in-situ formation of HA in CMC matrix and the weakening of intermolecular interactions after thermal treatment were depicted from FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 a,b). In the spectrum of CMC-HA, the characteristic bands of CMC and HA were found confirming the formation of CMC-HA nanocomposite. Moreover, the band at 1622cm−1 (Fig. 2b) assigned the carboxyl (COO−) groups of CMC was shifted to higher wavenumber at 1634cm−1 (Fig. 2a) confirming the electrostatic interaction between Ca2+ ions and carboxyl groups of CMC driven the in-situ mineralization of HA (Guo et al., 2016; Pasqui et al., 2014). In this process, Ca2+ions were initially attached to anionic anchoring (COO−) sites of CMC by electrostatic interactions. Then, phosphate (PO4 3−) ions react with calcium (Ca2+) ions and formed hydroxyapatite (HA) in the CMC matrix (Kumar, Mohaideem, Alariqi, & Singh, 2010). Furthermore, the hydroxyl (OH) band of CMC and HA were overlapped; and gave a broad band at 3444cm−1 indicates the H-bonding between CMC and HA. In the spectrum of CDs-CMC-HA, we have not found any additional bands; because CDs were formed from CMC having same functional groups as in CMC. But, the broadness of OH band was reduced which may be due to weakening of intermolecular interactions after continuous thermal treatment for 8h.

The XRD patterns of nanocomposites were given in Fig. 2c. In these patterns, we found all the characteristic peaks of HA at 2θ°∼26°, 28°, 32°, 34°, 40°, 47°, 50°, 56° and 64° corresponding to (002), (102), (211), (202), (310), (222), (004), (213) and (511) planes as per JCPDS card number 09-0432. These broad peaks suggest the nanocrystalline nature of HA in the composites. In these patterns, we have not observed any positional shifts in peaks; confirming that the incorporation of CMC and formation of CDs does not affect the crystalline phase of HA.

The thermal stability of synthesized nanocomposites was analyzed by TGA, shown in Fig. 2d. The thermogram of CMC-HA nanocomposite exhibited two thermal events; first event from 50°C to 190°C attributes the loss of adsorbed water molecules and the second event between 190°C −600°C represents the thermal decomposition of CMC (Guo et al., 2015). The curve nature of CDs-CMC-HA is same as CMC-HA nanocomposite. In CDs-CMC-HA curve, we observed that the decomposition of associated organic moieties was started at 230°C and finished at 600°C. It indicates that the thermal stability of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite was increased compared to CMC-HA after carbonization. The weight loss in CMC-HA and CDs-CMC-HA was 12% and 7% respectively; presented the carbonization of 5% CMC due to thermal treatment.

The microstructure and size of the HA nanoparticles in the synthesized nanocomposites were examined by FESEM and HRTEM; presented in Fig. 3 . In both nanocomposites, the morphology of HA nanoparticles are typical needle like with 60±15nm length and 20±10nm width. In CMC-HA nanocomposite (Fig. 3a,b), we observed cohesively organized HA nanoparticles in CMC matrix with a systematic arrangement; shows an effective interaction between CMC and HA. But in CDs-CMC-HA (Fig. 3c,d), the arrangement was disturbed after heat treatment indicates the weakening of interaction between CMC and HA. The carbonization of CMC after continuous thermal treatment might be responsible for weakening of interaction. In high resolution TEM image of CDs-CMC-HA (inset of Fig. 3d), the small spherical particles of size less than 10nm [shown by yellow arrows] were found onto the surface of HA nanoparticles might be recognized as carbon dots which were absent in CMC-HA nanocomposite (inset of Fig. 3b). Recently, Guo et al. (2016) synthesized HA/CDs composite and they prove the attachment of CDs upon HA surface by similar way.

The hydrodynamic particle size of the synthesized nanocomposites was determined by DLS study. Results (Fig. 3c,f) showed that the size of CMC-HA and CDs-CMC-HA was about 275±30nm and 150±20nm, respectively. The CDs-CMC-HA has less particle size than CMC-HA due to carbonization of CMC at 140°C; as a consequence the hydrodynamic particle size of CDs-CMC-HA was reduced.

The photoluminescence (PL) study of HA, CMC-HA and CDs-CMC-HA were carried out and presented in Fig. 4 a. We have not found any emission band in the spectra of HA and CMC-HA nanocomposite. However, CDs-CMC-HA exhibited a broad emission band centered at 440nm upon excited with 340nm wavelength. This observance confirmed the formation of fluorescent nanomaterial i.e. CDs from CMC by heat treatment at 140°C for 8h. We also collected the PL spectra of the CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite at different excitation from 300nm to 390nm (Fig. 4b). It exhibited the excitation dependent emission behavior of nanocomposite due to the presence of multiple-oxygen containing functional groups in CDs. These functional groups induce numerous localized energy levels within the n-π* gap, which decentralized the excited electrons (Zhang et al., 2016). Furthermore, the stability of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite was measured at 340nm excitation for different time periods and presented in Fig. S1 (Supporting information (SI)). It demonstrated that the CDs conjugated nanocomposite is highly stable after long term excitation. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs-CMC-HA was also determined by measuring PL decay and found 478ns (Fig. S2).

The intercellular uptake of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite by MDA-MB-468 cells was examined under fluorescence microscope (350nm excitation) at different incubation time (Fig. 5 ). It was observed that the nanocomposite was easily internalized into the cells and enlightens the cells with bright blue color. Fascinatingly the blue color was intensified with increasing incubation time; indicating that more nanoparticles were internalized into the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro DOX loading/release study and anticancer activity assay
ce:section.ce:para: DOX is a cationic anticancer drug. It can be efficiently loaded in anionic molecules/particles by electrostatic interactions and shows high drug loading efficiency (Guo et al., 2015; Verma et al., 2016). In this study, we have measured the surface charge (zeta potential) of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite in aqueous medium (Fig. S3 (SI)) and found that the nanocomposite possess negative charge (-19.7mV). After drug loading experiment, we found that the DOX loading content and entrapment efficiency in CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposites are 36.6% and 88.35% respectively. These high DOX loading content and entrapment efficiency of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite is due to the presence of carboxylic (COO−) group onto the surface of nanocomposite and bound electrostatistically with positively charged DOX molecule (Guo et al., 2015). The in-vitro release study of DOX from CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposites were performed at pseudo-physiological condition (pH∼7.4 and temp.∼37°C) with constant shaking. The drug release rate from DOX loaded nanocomposites was examined for predetermined time interval, which is shown in Fig. 6 a. It can be seen that 23% and 35% DOX was released from the CDs-CMC-HA after 24h and 48h at pH 7.4. The drug loading and release profile indicate that the DOX molecules were bound with CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite through electrostatic interaction with carboxyl group and released by a diffusion-controlled mechanism (Guo et al., 2015).

In order to evaluate the anticancer activity of DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite; in-vitro cytotoxicity of CDs-CMC-HA and DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA against MDA-MB-468 cells was studied by using MTT assay. The viability of MDA-MB-468 cells after treatment with different concentrations (5μgmL−1, 10μgmL−1, 15μgmL−1, 25μgmL−1, 50μgmL−1 and 100μgmL−1) of CDs-CMC-HA and DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite for 24h were presented in Fig. 6b. It was observed that CDs-CMC-HA has nontoxic effect on cancer cells. On the other hand, DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA shows dose dependent toxicity with 92%, 84%, 75%, 65%, 40% and 15% viable cells at 5μgmL−1, 10μgmL−1, 15μgmL−1, 25μgmL−1, 50μgmL−1 and 100μgmL−1 concentrations, respectively. These results indicate that the DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA successfully delivers the drug inside the cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro cell viability and cell differentiation study
ce:section.ce:para: In-vitro cell viability and proliferation of osteoblast like MG63 cells cultured with CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite were evaluated by MTT assay at 1, 4 and 7days of incubation (Fig. 7 a). In MTT assay, absorbance represents the metabolic activity of mitochondrial dehydrogenase of live cells. We found that the absorbance corresponds to live cells was increased with incubation time; indicating proliferation of cells. This is due to the presence of both bioactive HA and biocompatible CDs; and the decoration of CDs onto the surface of HA has an additional effect on the proliferation potential of cells (Gogai et al., 2016).

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an ectoenzyme express osteogenic cell differentiation in the early stage (Lee et al., 2015). We determined the ALP enzyme activity of MG63 cells cultured with osteogenic supplements (positive control) and CDs-CMC-HA (100μgmL−1) for 7days and 14 days; and normalized the data with total protein content (Fig. 7b). Results showed that the ALP enzyme activity of MG63 cells at day 14 was significantly increased (p< 0.01) in both test sample and positive control. This indicates that the CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite accelerates the cells for ALP production at a same rate as in positive control without any osteogenic agents. This positive effect of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite on MG63 cells was probably due to the presence of HA and the decoration of CDs onto HA surface facilitated better cell-material interactions and accelerates ALP activity of cells (Gogai et al., 2016).

The deposition of calcium phosphate in the extracellular matrix (ECM) is a late stage marker for osteogenic cell differentiation. ARS is commonly used for detecting calcium in the deposited mineral (Lee et al., 2015). The extracellular mineralization ability of MG63 cells in presence of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite was assessed and compared with positive control (osteogenic media) (Fig. 7c). At day 7, a very few red stained mineralized nodules were found in positive control and test sample. But, the color became more intense at day 14 and day 21; indicating that the large amount of calcium phosphate was deposited with time. VKS was also performed to monitor the mineralized nodules of the differentiated cells; it is specifically stain the carbonate or phosphate of deposits (Lee et al., 2015). Result (Fig. 7d) is consistent with alizarin staining, showed dense brown mineralized nodules at day 21 in both positive control and test sample. It indicates that the CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite has ability to promote MG63 cells for bone regeneration without any osteogenic supplements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Fe3+ ions with CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite to different metal ions was investigated and presented in Fig. 8 a,b. In presence of Fe3+ ions, significant quenching in fluorescent intensity was observed; showing specific sensitivity of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite towards ferric ion over other metal ions [Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Pb2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ni2+]. The selective and sensitive response of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite to Fe3+ ion was due to the presence of large number of oxygen containing functional groups particularly carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in CDs. Fe3+ ions have high affinity to these oxygenated functional groups of CDs and formed a complex (Wang et al., 2016). Due to this complex formation, the electrons were transferred from excited state of CDs to the d-orbitals of Fe3+ ions and showed quenched phenomenon (Song et al., 2014).

In Fig. 8c, the quenching of PL intensity of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite with increasing Fe3+ ions concentration (0–10μM) was observed upon excited with 340nm wavelength. The decreased fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear response with the Fe3+ ions concentration from 0.2 to 1.0μM. There is good correlation (R2 =0.98) between the relative intensity (F0/F) and the concentration of Fe3+ ion (Fig. 8d). The limit of detection is estimated from calibration curve and can be depicted as F0/F=1.171[Fe3+]+0.82. The CDs-CMC-HA could sense the Fe3+ ion with a detection limit of 55nM.

The present work demonstrated the successful fabrication of fluorescent enabling carbon dots conjugated CMC-HA nanocomposite through a simple one pot method. The synthesized CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite exhibited an intense blue emission at 440nm with high stability. In this report, the functionality of synthesized CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite has been explored in three directions particularly in bone tissue engineering, drug delivery and ion sensing arena. Synthesized nanocomposite showed cell differentiation ability with high ALP activity of MG63 cells followed by extracellular mineralization. Moreover, the biological application of synthesized nanocomposite has been extended to cell imaging and drug delivery. The anticancer drug DOX has been successfully loaded in CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposites with high loading content (36.6%) and loading efficiency (88.35%). Furthermore, the as-synthesized CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions over other metal ions. The synthesized CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite has potential to be used multifariously in biomedical field such as bone tissue engineering, drug delivery and ion sensing arena; and opens up the way to apply synergistically.
The authors declare no conflict of interest

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge In house project support group (iPSG) (OLP-0231), CSIR- National Metallurgical Laboratory for financial support and research facilities. The authors also like to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines) [IIT-ISM], Dhanbad for providing characterization facilities. Author (Chandrani Sarkar) acknowledges University Grants Commission, India for granting Fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2017.11.091.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Photostability test of CDs conjugated CMC-HA nanocomposite; PL decay curve of CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite; Zeta potential measurement of CDs-CMC-HA in aqueous medium at pH∼7; Digital images of (a) HA nanoparticles, (b) CMC-HA, (c) CDs-CMC-HA and (d) DOX loaded CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite; Digital images of (a) free DOX solution and (b) DOX after loading in CDs-CMC-HA nanocomposite.
